article_title: (no title)
objection: It would seem that the habit of knowledge acquired in this life does not remain in the soul separated from the body: for the Apostle says: "Knowledge shall be destroyed" (1 Cor. 13:8).
reply: The Apostle is not speaking of knowledge as a habit, but as to the act of knowing; and hence he says, in proof of the assertion quoted, "Now, I know in part."
prompt: It looks like the common way of what we know that we got in this life does not stay in the inside part when it is away from the outside part: because the writer of the early letter says: "what we know will end"
response: The writer is not talking about what we know as a common way, but about the time when we do know; and so he says, as a show of the idea he said, "Now I know some."

article_title: Whether God Knows Things Other Than Himself?
objection: Further, the object understood is the perfection of the one who understands. If therefore God understands other things besides Himself, something else will be the perfection of God, and will be nobler than He; which is impossible.
reply: The object understood is a perfection of the one understanding not by its substance, but by its image, according to which it is in the intellect, as its form and perfection, as is said in De Anima iii. For "a stone is not in the soul, but its image." Now those things which are other than God are understood by God, inasmuch as the essence of God contains their images as above explained; hence it does not follow that there is any perfection in the divine intellect other than the divine essence.
prompt: Also, what is known is the best part of the one who knows. If the big one knows other things besides himself, then something else will be the best part of the big one and will be more good than he, which cannot be.
response: What is known is a best part of the one who knows not by what it is made of but by its picture, as it is in the mind, as its shape and best part, as a book says. For a rock is not in the mind but its picture. Now those things that are not the big one are known by the big one because what the big one really is holds their pictures as we said; so it does not mean that there is any best part in the mind of the big one that is not the big one itself.

article_title: Whether the Perfections of All Things Are in God?
objection: It seems that the perfections of all things are not in God. For God is simple, as shown above (Q. 3, A. 7); whereas the perfections of things are many and diverse. Therefore the perfections of all things are not in God.
reply: Even as the sun (as Dionysius remarks, (Div. Nom. v)), while remaining one and shining uniformly, contains within itself first and uniformly the substances of sensible things, and many and diverse qualities; a fortiori should all things in a kind of natural unity pre-exist in the cause of all things; and thus things diverse and in themselves opposed to each other, pre-exist in God as one, without injury to His simplicity. This suffices for the Reply to the Second Objection.
prompt: It looks like the good parts of all things are not in god. Because god is just one, as we saw before; but the good parts of things are many and different. So the good parts of all things are not in god.
response: Just like the sun, as the old man who said about the world says, stays one and gives light the same in all places, it holds inside it the first and same what things we can see are made of, and many different looks. In the same way, all things that are in a real being together should be there before the thing that makes all things; so things that are many and even different are there before god as one, and this does not hurt god being just one.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, the Master of the Sentences (ii, D, 11) says that the superior angels had long known the Mystery of the Incarnation, whereas the inferior angels did not know it until it was accomplished. Thus we find that on some of the angels inquiring, as it were, in ignorance: "Who is this King of glory?" other angels, who knew, answered: "The Lord of Hosts, He is the King of glory," as Dionysius expounds (Coel. Hier. vii). But this would not apply if the superior angels enlightened the inferior concerning all they know themselves. Therefore they do not do so.
reply: The Master's words are not to be understood as if the inferior angels were entirely ignorant of the Mystery of the Incarnation but that they did not know it as fully as the superior angels; and that they progressed in the knowledge of it afterwards when the Mystery was accomplished.
prompt: the teacher of the words says that the big angels had long known the secret thing of god be a person, while the small angels did not know it until it was done. so we see that some angels ask, like they do not know: who is this big good king? other angels who know say: the god of many groups, he is the big good king, as the old wise teacher from a far place says. but this would not be true if the big angels show the small angels all they know by them. therefore they do not do that.
response: the teacher of the words does not mean that the small angels do not know the secret thing of god be a person, but that they do not know it as much as the big angels and they learn more about it later after it is done.

article_title: Whether the Father and the Son Are One Principle of the Holy Ghost?
objection: Further, the Son is not one with the Father more than is the Holy Ghost. But the Holy Ghost and the Father are not one principle as regards any other divine person. Therefore neither are the Father and the Son.
reply: It is not by reason of relative properties that we speak of similitude or dissimilitude in God, but by reason of the essence. Hence, as the Father is not more like to Himself than He is to the Son; so likewise neither is the Son more like to the Father than is the Holy Ghost.
prompt: More, the second god person is not more the same as the first god person than the third god person is. But the third god person and the first god person are not the same thing for any other god person. So the first god person and the second god person are not the same.
response: It is not as different things we see that we talk about be like or not like in god but as what something really is. So as the first god person is not more like himself than he is to the second god person also the second god person is not more like the first god person than the third god person is.

article_title: Whether There Is Trinity in God?
objection: Further, all that exists in God exists in the unity of the divine essence; because God is His own essence. Therefore, if Trinity exists in God, it exists in the unity of the divine essence; and thus in God there would be three essential unities; which is heresy.
reply: In the divine Trinity is to be understood both number and the persons numbered. So when we say, "Trinity in Unity," we do not place number in the unity of the essence, as if we meant three times one; but we place the Persons numbered in the unity of nature; as the supposita of a nature are said to exist in that nature. On the other hand, we say "Unity in Trinity"; meaning that the nature is in its supposita.
prompt: All that is in god is in the one of what god is as god is his own what he is. So if the three people are in god they are in the one of what god is and so in god there is three one which is a bad idea.
response: In the god three people idea we think about both a number and the people with that number. So when we say 'three people in one' we do not put the number in the one of what god is as if we said three and one but we put the three people in the one of the kind as the people that are said to be of a kind are said to be in that kind and we say 'one in three people' that means the kind is in its people.

article_title: Whether There Are Real Relations in God?
objection: Further, the divine generation proceeds by way of an intelligible word. But the relations following upon the operation of the intellect are logical relations. Therefore paternity and filiation in God, consequent upon generation, are only logical relations.
reply: Relations which result from the mental operation alone in the objects understood are logical relations only, inasmuch as reason observes them as existing between two objects perceived by the mind. Those relations, however, which follow the operation of the intellect, and which exist between the word intellectually proceeding and the source whence it proceeds, are not logical relations only, but are real relations; inasmuch as the intellect and the reason are real things, and are really related to that which proceeds from them intelligibly; as a corporeal thing is related to that which proceeds from it corporeally. Thus paternity and filiation are real relations in God.
prompt: Also god making works by a clear word. But the connections that come after the mind works are thinking connections. So father and child links in god that come after making are only thinking connections.
response: Connections that happen just from the mind work on things we think about are only thinking connections because the mind sees them as being between two things the mind sees. But the connections that happen after the mind works and that are between the clear word that comes from the mind and the place it comes from are not only thinking connections but true connections because the mind and the thinking are true things and they join to what comes from them in a clear way as a body thing joins to what comes from it in a body way. So father and child links are true connections in god.

article_title: Whether There Is Falsity in the Senses?
objection: It seems that falsity is not in the senses. For Augustine says (De Vera Relig. 33): "If all the bodily senses report as they are affected, I do not know what more we can require from them." Thus it seems that we are not deceived by the senses; and therefore that falsity is not in them.
reply: The affection of sense is its sensation itself. Hence, from the fact that sense reports as it is affected, it follows that we are not deceived in the judgment by which we judge that we experience sensation. Since, however, sense is sometimes affected erroneously of that object, it follows that it sometimes reports erroneously of that object; and thus we are deceived by sense about the object, but not about the fact of sensation.
prompt: It looks like false is not in the things we see, hear, feel, taste, or smell. The old teacher of the old group says, "If all body senses tell us what they feel, I do not see what else we need from them." So it looks like we are not tricked by the senses, and so false is not in them.
response: A sense feeling is just the feeling itself. So if a sense tells us what it feels, we are not tricked about the fact that we feel. But at times a sense is wrong about what it is feeling, so it can tell us the wrong thing about the thing we are feeling. So we can be tricked by a sense about the thing but not about the fact that we felt.

article_title: Whether Whatever God Does Outside the Natural Order Is Miraculous?
objection: Further, the word miracle is derived from admiration. Now admiration concerns things manifest to the senses. But sometimes things happen outside the order of nature, which are not manifest to the senses; as when the Apostles were endowed with knowledge without studying or being taught. Therefore not everything that occurs outside the order of nature is miraculous.
reply: The knowledge of the Apostles, although not manifest in itself, yet was made manifest in its effect, from which it was shown to be wonderful.
prompt: Also, the word strange good thing comes from wonder. Wonder is about things shown to eyes. But sometimes things happen outside the way the world works that are not shown to eyes; like when the people who help were given what is known without learning or being told. So not every thing that happens outside the way the world works is a strange good thing.
response: The what is known by the people who help, even if it was not shown by itself, was shown by what it did, and that made it look like a wonder.

article_title: Whether This Name "God" Is Communicable?
objection: Further, only proper names are not communicable. Now this name "God" is not a proper, but an appellative noun; which appears from the fact that it has a plural, according to the text, "I have said, You are gods" (Ps. 81:6). Therefore this name "God" is communicable.
reply: This name "God" is an appellative name, and not a proper name, for it signifies the divine nature in the possessor; although God Himself in reality is neither universal nor particular. For names do not follow upon the mode of being in things, but upon the mode of being as it is in our mind. And yet it is incommunicable according to the truth of the thing, as was said above concerning the name "sun."
prompt: also only own name not can be give. this name god is not an own name but a kind name because it has many as the words say i have said you are many god (ps 81:6). so this name god can be give.
response: this name god is a kind name not an own name because it show the god part in the one who has it even if god is not for all or one. a name is not about how things are but about how we think of them but it not can be give because that is the right way of the thing like we said before about the name sun.

article_title: Whether the First Man Knew All Things?
objection: Further, individuals of the same species have the same way of arriving at perfection. Now other men have not, from the beginning, knowledge of all things, but they acquire it in the course of time according to their capacity. Therefore neither did Adam know all things when he was first created.
reply: To Adam, as being the first man, was due a degree of perfection which was not due to other men, as is clear from what is above explained.
prompt: Also people of the same kind have the same way to get the best. Now other men do not, from the first, know all things, but they get it as time goes on as much as they can. So the first man did not know all things when he was first made.
response: The first man got some of the best that other men do not get, as the words before show.

article_title: Whether an Inferior Angel Can Enlighten a Superior Angel?
objection: Further, one angel enlightens the other to whom he turns, as was above explained (A. 1). But since this turning to another is voluntary, the highest angel can turn to the lowest passing over the others. Therefore he can enlighten him immediately; and thus the latter can enlighten his superiors.
reply: An angel turns voluntarily to enlighten another angel, but the angel's will is ever regulated by the Divine law which made the order in the angels.
prompt: Also one god's friend makes light for another god's friend when he turns to them as we said before. But because this turn is by own will the most high god's friend can turn to the most low god's friend go past the others. So he can make light for the low one right away and then the low one can make light for the higher ones.
response: A god's friend turns by own will to make light for another god's friend but the friend's will is always under god's law that set the group of god's friends.

article_title: Whether There Are Several Powers of the Soul?
objection: Further, to operate belongs to what is in act. But by the one essence of the soul, man has actual existence in the different degrees of perfection, as we have seen above (Q. 76, AA. 3, 4). Therefore by the one power of the soul he performs operations of various degrees.
reply: One thing has one substantial existence, but may have several operations. So there is one essence of the soul, with several powers.
prompt: Also, to do things is part of what is in action. But because the spirit has one what it really is, a person has real being in many levels of good, as we have said before. So because the spirit has one ability, a person does things at many levels.
response: One thing has one real being, but may do many things. So the spirit has one what it really is, with many ways to act.

article_title: Whether Anyone in This Life Can See the Essence of God?
objection: It seems that one can in this life see the Divine essence. For Jacob said: "I have seen God face to face" (Gen. 32:30). But to see Him face to face is to see His essence, as appears from the words: "We see now in a glass and in a dark manner, but then face to face" (1 Cor. 13:12).
reply: According to Dionysius (Coel. Hier. iv) a man is said in the Scriptures to see God in the sense that certain figures are formed in the senses or imagination, according to some similitude representing in part the divinity. So when Jacob says, "I have seen God face to face," this does not mean the Divine essence, but some figure representing God. And this is to be referred to some high mode of prophecy, so that God seems to speak, though in an imaginary vision; as will later be explained (II-II, Q. 174) in treating of the degrees of prophecy. We may also say that Jacob spoke thus to designate some exalted intellectual contemplation, above the ordinary state.
prompt: It looks like a person can in this life see what God really is. The man from the old story said, "I have seen God face to face." But to see Him face to face means to see what He really is, as the words say, "We see now in a mirror and in a dim way, but later we will see face to face."
response: The old wise man from a far place says the old book says a person can see God in a way that some form is made in the eyes or mind picture, like a small part of what God is. So when the man from the old story says, "I have seen God face to face," it does not mean he saw what God really is, but a form that looks like God. This is about a high way of talk from God, where God seems to speak in a mind picture; this will be told later when we talk about levels of talk from God. We can also say the man used the words to point to a high thinking over the normal life.

article_title: Whether the Will Desires of Necessity, Whatever It Desires?
objection: Further, the object of the will is compared to the will as the mover to the thing movable. But the movement of the movable necessarily follows the mover. Therefore it seems that the will's object moves it of necessity.
reply: The mover, then, of necessity causes movement in the thing movable, when the power of the mover exceeds the thing movable, so that its entire capacity is subject to the mover. But as the capacity of the will regards the universal and perfect good, its capacity is not subjected to any individual good. And therefore it is not of necessity moved by it.
prompt: And what the will wants is like the first thing that makes a other thing move. But the move of the other thing always comes after the first thing. So it looks like the thing the will wants makes it move because it must.
response: The first thing that makes a move, when it must, makes the other thing move when the first thing is strong enough for the other, so all power of the other is under the first. But the power of the will is about the great good, not about any small good. So it does not have to move because of a small good.

article_title: Whether an Angel's Knowledge Is Discursive?
objection: Further, Isidore (De sum. bono i, 10) says that "demons learn more things by experience." But experimental knowledge is discursive: for, "one experience comes of many remembrances, and one universal from many experiences," as Aristotle observes (Poster. ii; Metaph. vii). Therefore an angel's knowledge is discursive.
reply: Experience is affirmed of angels and demons simply by way of similitude, forasmuch as they know sensible things which are present, yet without any discursion withal.
prompt: The old writer says that the bad being learns more things by what they see. But what we know from what we see is put together in many small parts, for one thing we see comes from many memories, and one big idea comes from many things we see, as the wise old teacher says. So the good being has what we know in many small parts.
response: We say that good and bad being also have what we see, because they know things we can sense that are here, but they do not put them together in many small parts.

article_title: Whether Every Being Is Good?
objection: Further, the Philosopher notes (Metaph. iii) that "in mathematics goodness does not exist." But mathematics are entities; otherwise there would be no science of mathematics. Therefore not every being is good.
reply: Mathematical entities do not subsist as realities; because they would be in some sort good if they subsisted; but they have only logical existence, inasmuch as they are abstracted from motion and matter; thus they cannot have the aspect of an end, which itself has the aspect of moving another. Nor is it repugnant that there should be in some logical entity neither goodness nor form of goodness; since the idea of being is prior to the idea of goodness, as was said in the preceding article.
prompt: Also, the old teacher says (thinking about what is real part three) that 'in math good does not exist.' But math are things; if they were not, there would be no study of math. So not every thing is good.
response: Math things are not real things; if they were real they would be a kind of good; but they only exist in the mind, because we take them away from moving and stuff; so they can not have a goal that moves other things. It is not bad that a mind thing has no good or kind of good; because the idea of a thing comes before the idea of good, as said in the earlier part.

article_title: Whether Goodness Is Prior in Idea to Being?
objection: Further, not only is existence desirable, but life, knowledge, and many other things besides. Thus it seems that existence is a particular appetible, and goodness a universal appetible. Therefore, absolutely, goodness is prior in idea to being.
reply: Life, wisdom, and the like, are desirable only so far as they are actual. Hence, in each one of them some sort of being is desired. And thus nothing can be desired except being; and consequently nothing is good except being.
prompt: Also, not only is being wanted, but life, know, and many other things too. So it seems like being is a some wanted thing, and good is an all wanted thing. So for sure good comes before being in thought.
response: Life, think, and things like that are wanted only when they are real. So every one of them some part of being is wanted. And so nothing can be wanted except being; and so nothing is good except being.

article_title: Whether Any Name Can Be Applied to God in Its Literal Sense?
objection: It seems that no name is applied literally to God. For all names which we apply to God are taken from creatures; as was explained above (A. 1). But the names of creatures are applied to God metaphorically, as when we say, God is a stone, or a lion, or the like. Therefore names are applied to God in a metaphorical sense.
reply: There are some names which signify these perfections flowing from God to creatures in such a way that the imperfect way in which creatures receive the divine perfection is part of the very signification of the name itself as "stone" signifies a material being, and names of this kind can be applied to God only in a metaphorical sense. Other names, however, express these perfections absolutely, without any such mode of participation being part of their signification as the words "being," "good," "living," and the like, and such names can be literally applied to God.
prompt: It looks like no name can be used for God in a real way. All the names we use for God come from things. As we said before, the names of things are used for God in a picture way, like when we say God is a stone or a lion. So the names we use for God are only in a picture way.
response: Some names tell about the good things that come from God to things in a way that the not best way that things get the good thing is part of what the name means, as the word stone means a stuff thing, and names like that can only be used for God in a picture way. Other names tell about these good things all, without that part in their meaning, like the words being, good, alive, and names like those can be used for God in a real way.

article_title: Whether Any Other Procession Exists in God Besides That of the Word?
objection: Further, if any other procession but the intelligible procession of the Word existed in God, it could only be the procession of love, which is by the operation of the will. But such a procession is identified with the intelligible procession of the intellect, inasmuch as the will in God is the same as His intellect (Q. 19, A. 1). Therefore in God there is no other procession but the procession of the Word.
reply: Though will and intellect are not diverse in God, nevertheless the nature of will and intellect requires the processions belonging to each of them to exist in a certain order. For the procession of love occurs in due order as regards the procession of the Word; since nothing can be loved by the will unless it is conceived in the intellect. So as there exists a certain order of the Word to the principle whence He proceeds, although in God the substance of the intellect and its concept are the same; so, although in God the will and the intellect are the same, still, inasmuch as love requires by its very nature that it proceed only from the concept of the intellect, there is a distinction of order between the procession of love and the procession of the Word in God.
prompt: Also, if any other coming out other than the clear coming out of the Word were in God, it could only be a coming out of care, which comes from the action of the choice part. But that coming out is the same as the clear coming out of the thinking part, because the choice part in God is the same as His thinking part. So in God there is no other coming out except the coming out of the Word.
response: Even though the choice part and the thinking part are not different in God, the way they work still makes the coming out that belongs to each have a right order. The coming out of care comes after the coming out of the Word, because the choice part cannot care for something that the thinking part has not first thought of. So there is a right order from the Word to the first origin it comes from, even if in God the thing that is the thinking part and its idea are the same; and even if in God the choice part and the thinking part are the same, because care must come only from the idea of the thinking part, there is a different order between the coming out of care and the coming out of the Word in God.

article_title: Whether the Word "Person" Should Be Said of God?
objection: It would seem that the name "person" should not be said of God. For Dionysius says (Div. Nom.): "No one should ever dare to say or think anything of the supersubstantial and hidden Divinity, beyond what has been divinely expressed to us by the oracles." But the name "person" is not expressed to us in the Old or New Testament. Therefore "person" is not to be applied to God.
reply: Although the word "person" is not found applied to God in Scripture, either in the Old or New Testament, nevertheless what the word signifies is found to be affirmed of God in many places of Scripture; as that He is the supreme self-subsisting being, and the most perfectly intelligent being. If we could speak of God only in the very terms themselves of Scripture, it would follow that no one could speak about God in any but the original language of the Old or New Testament. The urgency of confuting heretics made it necessary to find new words to express the ancient faith about God. Nor is such a kind of novelty to be shunned; since it is by no means profane, for it does not lead us astray from the sense of Scripture. The Apostle warns us to avoid "profane novelties of words" (1 Tim. 6:20).
prompt: It looks like we should not call God a who can think and act. The old man says that we should not say or think about the big not shown thing to do with God except what the words have told us. But we do not see the words who can think and act in the old book or new book. So we should not use the words who can think and act for God.
response: Even though we do not see the words who can think and act used for God in the old book or new book, the idea about those words is said about God many times in the books; for God is the top own real thing and the most very thinking thing. If we could only talk about God using the same words of the books, then we could only speak in the first words of the old book or new book. The need to talk against wrong people made us need new words to talk about the old idea about God. Using new words is not bad; it does not take us away from what the books mean. The old man tells us not to use bad new words.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, nothing is made for the sake of that which is inferior to itself, "since the end is better than the means" (Topic. iii). But the lights are nobler than the earth. Therefore they were not made "to enlighten it."
reply: Light was given to the earth for the service of man, who, by reason of his soul, is nobler than the heavenly bodies. Nor is it untrue to say that a higher creature may be made for the sake of a lower, considered not in itself, but as ordained to the good of the universe.
prompt: Also, no thing is made for the sake of a thing that is lower than it, because the end is better than the means. But the bright things in the sky are higher than the ground. So they were not made to give light to the ground.
response: The bright thing was given to the ground so that people can use it, because people, by their thought, are higher than the bright things in the sky. It is also true to say that a higher thing can be made for the sake of a lower thing, not by seeing the lower thing alone, but as part of the good of all.

article_title: Whether the Soul Knows Bodies Through the Intellect?
objection: Further, as sense is to the intelligible, so is the intellect to the sensible. But the soul can by no means, through the senses, understand spiritual things, which are intelligible. Therefore by no means can it, through the intellect, know bodies, which are sensible.
reply: As Augustine says (De Civ. Dei xxii, 29), it is not correct to say that as the sense knows only bodies so the intellect knows only spiritual things; for it follows that God and the angels would not know corporeal things. The reason of this diversity is that the lower power does not extend to those things that belong to the higher power; whereas the higher power operates in a more excellent manner those things which belong to the lower power.
prompt: Also, just like the sense is for what we can think about, the mind is for what we can see or touch. But the self cannot, by the sense, know spirit things, which are what we can think about. So the self cannot, by the mind, know body things, which are what we can see or touch.
response: The great old church teacher says it is not right to say that because the sense knows only body things, the mind knows only spirit things; for then god and angels would not know body things. The reason for this is that the lower part cannot reach the things that are of the higher part, while the higher part works in a better way on the things that are of the lower part.

article_title: Whether Any Creature Can Be Like God?
objection: Further, likeness implies comparison. But there can be no comparison between things in a different genus. Therefore neither can there be any likeness. Thus we do not say that sweetness is like whiteness. But no creature is in the same genus as God: since God is no genus, as shown above (Q. 3, A. 5). Therefore no creature is like God.
reply: God is not related to creatures as though belonging to a different genus, but as transcending every genus, and as the principle of all genera.
prompt: Also, being like means to compare. But you cannot compare things that are of a different type. So you cannot say any two things are like each other. For example we do not say a nice taste thing is like a white thing. But no animal is of the same type as the big maker: because the big maker is not a type, as shown before. So no animal is like the big maker.
response: The big maker is not a type of animal or a type of any other type. Instead the big maker is above every type, and is the main idea of all types.

article_title: Whether the Interior Senses Are Suitably Distinguished?
objection: Further, there is no need to assign an interior power of apprehension when the proper and exterior sense suffices. But the proper and exterior senses suffice for us to judge of sensible things; for each sense judges of its proper object. In like manner they seem to suffice for the perception of their own actions; for since the action of the sense is, in a way, between the power and its object, it seems that sight must be much more able to perceive its own vision, as being nearer to it, than the color; and in like manner with the other senses. Therefore for this there is no need to assign an interior power, called the common sense.
reply: The proper sense judges of the proper sensible by discerning it from other things which come under the same sense; for instance, by discerning white from black or green. But neither sight nor taste can discern white from sweet: because what discerns between two things must know both. Wherefore the discerning judgment must be assigned to the common sense; to which, as to a common term, all apprehensions of the senses must be referred: and by which, again, all the intentions of the senses are perceived; as when someone sees that he sees. For this cannot be done by the proper sense, which only knows the form of the sensible by which it is immuted, in which immutation the action of sight is completed, and from immutation follows another in the common sense which perceives the act of vision.
prompt: Also, there is no need to give an inside way to understand when the right outside sense is enough. The right outside senses are enough for us to decide about things we can see or feel; each sense decides about its own thing. In the same way they seem enough for us to see their own work; because the work of a sense is between the sense and the thing, it seems that seeing can see its own seeing better than it can see a color; and the same for the other senses. So there is no need to give an inside way, which we call all sense.
response: The right sense decides about its own thing by seeing the difference from other things that the same sense can see; for example, by seeing white different from black or green. But seeing or tasting cannot see white different from sweet, because to see the difference between two things you must know both. So the deciding about differences must be given to all sense; to it, like a common word, all thought of the senses must point, and by it again all the work of the senses is seen, like when a person sees that he sees. This cannot be done by the right sense, which only knows the shape of the thing we see by which it is changed, in which the change the work of seeing is finished, and from that change another part comes in all sense which sees the work of seeing.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, some are chosen to the life of grace who are not chosen to the life of glory; as it is clear from what is said: "Have not I chosen you twelve, and one of you is a devil?" (John 6:71). But the book of life is the inscription of the divine election, as stated above (A. 1). Therefore it applies also to the life of grace.
reply: The life of grace has the aspect, not of an end, but of something directed towards an end. Hence nobody is said to be chosen to the life of grace, except so far as the life of grace is directed to glory. For this reason those who, possessing grace, fail to obtain glory, are not said to be chosen simply, but relatively. Likewise they are not said to be written in the book of life simply, but relatively; that is to say, that it is in the ordination and knowledge of God that they are to have some relation to eternal life, according to their participation in grace.
prompt: Also, some are chosen for a life of good gift who are not chosen for a life of big good; as it is easy to see from what is said: 'Did I not choose twelve of you, and one of you is a devil?' (the man called John 6:71). But the book of life is the written by God's choosing, as said above (A. 1). So it works also for a life of good gift.
response: The life of good gift is not a last place but a way that goes to a last place. So no one is said to be chosen for a life of good gift if not that life goes to big good. For this reason those who have good gift but do not get big good are not said to be chosen all, but only in part. Also they are not said to be written in the book of life all, but only in part; that means that in God's choosing and what God knows they have some join to always life, as they share in good gift.

article_title: Whether All Things Are Subject to the Divine Government?
objection: It would seem that not all things are subject to the Divine government. For it is written (Eccles. 9:11): "I saw that under the sun the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the learned, nor favor to the skillful, but time and chance in all." But things subject to the Divine government are not ruled by chance. Therefore those things which are under the sun are not subject to the Divine government.
reply: These things are said to be under the sun which are generated and corrupted according to the sun's movement. In all such things we find chance: not that everything is casual which occurs in such things; but that in each one there is an element of chance. And the very fact that an element of chance is found in those things proves that they are subject to government of some kind. For unless corruptible things were governed by a higher being, they would tend to nothing definite, especially those which possess no kind of knowledge. So nothing would happen unintentionally; which constitutes the nature of chance. Wherefore to show how things happen by chance and yet according to the ordering of a higher cause, he does not say absolutely that he observes chance in all things, but "time and chance," that is to say, that defects may be found in these things according to some order of time.
prompt: It looks like not all things are under the rule of the one who made all. The book says: I saw that when the sun is, the run is not for the fast, the fight is not for the strong, the food is not for the smart, the money is not for the wise, the help is not for the good at work, but time and chance are in everything. But things that are under the rule of the one who made all are not run by chance. So the things that are under the sun are not under the rule of the one who made all.
response: Those things that are under the sun are things that are made and break as the sun moves. In all of those things we see chance. That does not mean everything is random in those things; it means that each thing has a part that is chance. And the fact that a chance part is in those things shows they are under some rule. If things that can break were not under the rule of the one who made all, they would not move toward any end, especially those that have no mind. So nothing would happen by chance, and that is what we call chance. That is why the book does not say that chance is in every thing all the time, but says time and chance, meaning that small problems can be in those things as part of time's order.

article_title: Whether the Human Soul Was Produced Before the Body?
objection: Further, the end is proportionate to the beginning. But in the end the soul outlasts the body. Therefore in the beginning it was created before the body.
reply: That the soul remains after the body, is due to a defect of the body, namely, death. Which defect was not due when the soul was first created.
prompt: Also, the finish is the same as the start. But at the finish the part of a person that thinks and feels stays longer than the body. So at the start it was made before the body.
response: The reason the part of a person that thinks and feels stays after the body is because the body has a problem, which is that it dies. That problem was not there when the part was first made.

article_title: (no title)
objection: It would seem that in the state of innocence children would have been born confirmed in righteousness. For Gregory says (Moral. iv) on the words of Job 3:13: "For now I should have been asleep, etc.": "If no sinful corruption had infected our first parent, he would not have begotten 'children of hell'; no children would have been born of him but such as were destined to be saved by the Redeemer." Therefore all would have been born confirmed in righteousness.
reply: If Adam had not sinned, he would not have begotten "children of hell" in the sense that they would contract from him sin which is the cause of hell: yet by sinning of their own free-will they could have become "children of hell." If, however, they did not become "children of hell" by falling into sin, this would not have been owing to their being confirmed in righteousness, but to Divine Providence preserving them free from sin.
prompt: It would seem that when there was no bad, kids would be born already good. The old guide says on the words of a story about a man who said: 'If no bad had hit our first parent, he would not have made kids of a bad place; no kids would be born of him except those who would be saved by the one who saves.' So all would be born already good.
response: If the first parent had not done a bad act, he would not have made kids of a bad place in the way that they get bad from him that lead to a bad place; yet by doing a bad act of their own pick they could become kids of a bad place. If they did not become kids of a bad place by picking bad, this would not be because they were made sure they are good, but because god care keep them free from bad.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, since desire is of a thing not possessed but possible to have, whoever desires to know anything is in potentiality thereto. But it is said (1 Pet. 1:12): "On Whom the angels desire to look." Therefore the angel's intellect is sometimes in potentiality.
reply: Such desire on the part of the angels does not exclude the object desired, but weariness thereof. Or they are said to desire the vision of God with regard to fresh revelations, which they receive from God to fit them for the tasks which they have to perform.
prompt: Also, because want is about something you do not have but could have, any person who wants to learn something is in a maybe state of getting it. But a book says that the sky helpers want to look at someone. So the sky helper mind is sometimes in a maybe state.
response: That want by the sky helpers does not mean they do not want the thing, but they are tired of it. Or they want to see the big one with new news, which they get from the big one to help them do the jobs they must do.

article_title: Whether There Are More Than Two Processions in God?
objection: It would seem that there are more than two processions in God. As knowledge and will are attributed to God, so is power. Therefore, if two processions exist in God, of intellect and will, it seems that there must also be a third procession of power.
reply: Power is the principle whereby one thing acts on another. Hence it is that external action points to power. Thus the divine power does not imply the procession of a divine person; but is indicated by the procession therefrom of creatures.
prompt: It looks like there are more than two ways something comes out of God. Because we say God has what is known and wants, we also say God has power. So if there are two ways something comes out of God, the mind way and the want way, it looks like there must be a third way, the power way.
response: Power is the way that one thing does something to another thing. So when we see something doing something to another thing, it shows power. So the God power does not mean there is a third coming out of a God person; it just shows up as the way that things that come from God act.

article_title: Whether the Angels Were Created in Grace?
objection: Further, grace comes midway between nature and glory. But the angels were not beatified in their creation. Therefore it seems that they were not created in grace; but that they were first created in nature only, and then received grace, and that last of all they were beatified.
reply: Although in the order of nature grace comes midway between nature and glory, nevertheless, in the order of time, in created nature, glory is not simultaneous with nature; because glory is the end of the operation of nature helped by grace. But grace stands not as the end of operation, because it is not of works, but as the principle of right operation. Therefore it was fitting for grace to be given straightway with nature.
prompt: Also, good help is in the part between the way things are made and great honor. But the good ones were not made very good when they were first made. So it looks like they were not first made with good help; instead they were first only made the way things are made, then got good help, and at the end they were made very good.
response: Even if in the way things are made good help is in the part between the way things are made and great honor, still in true time great honor does not happen at the same time as the way things are made, because great honor is the last part of the work of the way things are made with help of good help. But good help is not the last part of work, because it is not about work, but is the big idea of good work. So it was right for good help to be given right away with the way things are made.

article_title: Whether the Will Desires Something of Necessity?
objection: Further, the rational powers, according to the Philosopher (Metaph. viii, 2), extend to opposite things. But the will is a rational power, because, as he says (De Anima iii, 9), "the will is in the reason." Therefore the will extends to opposite things, and therefore it is determined to nothing of necessity.
reply: The will, so far as it desires a thing naturally, corresponds rather to the intellect as regards natural principles than to the reason, which extends to opposite things. Wherefore in this respect it is rather an intellectual than a rational power.
prompt: Also, the thinking powers, as the old teacher says in his book about what is real, reach to different things. But the will is a thinking power, because, as he says in his book about the mind, the will is in the thinking. So the will reaches to different things, and so it is not set to any one thing that must happen.
response: The will, when it want a thing in a natural way, is more like the mind that works with rule than like the thinking that reach to different things. So in this way it is more a mind thing than a thinking strength.

article_title: Whether "Love" Is the Proper Name of the Holy Ghost?
objection: Further, the Holy Ghost is a subsisting person, but love is not used to signify a subsisting person, but rather an action passing from the lover to the beloved. Therefore Love is not the proper name of the Holy Ghost.
reply: Although to understand, and to will, and to love signify actions passing on to their objects, nevertheless they are actions that remain in the agents, as stated above (Q. 14, A. 4), yet in such a way that in the agent itself they import a certain relation to their object. Hence, love also in ourselves is something that abides in the lover, and the word of the heart is something abiding in the speaker; yet with a relation to the thing expressed by word, or loved. But in God, in whom there is nothing accidental, there is more than this; because both Word and Love are subsistent. Therefore, when we say that the Holy Ghost is the Love of the Father for the Son, or for something else; we do not mean anything that passes into another, but only the relation of love to the beloved; as also in the Word is imported the relation of the Word to the thing expressed by the Word.
prompt: Also, the good spirit is a real person, but love is not used to mean a real person, it is an action that goes from the one who loves to the one who is loved. So love is not the right name for the good spirit.
response: Even if we think of understand, want, and love as actions that go to a thing, those actions still stay inside the person who does them, as we said before. In that way the action of love lives inside the one who loves, and the word that comes from a heart lives inside the one who talks, while still point to the thing talk about or the thing that is loved. But in God, where all is true, love is more than just an action; it is itself a real thing. So when we say the good spirit is the love of the father for the son, we do not talk about love that moves from one thing to another, but about the love link that is inside God, just as the talk of God shows the link of the talk to the thing it talks about.

article_title: Whether Man by the Power of His Soul Can Change Corporeal Matter?
objection: It would seem that man by the power of his soul can change corporeal matter. For Gregory says (Dialog. ii, 30): "Saints work miracles sometimes by prayer, sometimes by their power: thus Peter, by prayer, raised the dead Tabitha to life, and by his reproof delivered to death the lying Ananias and Saphira." But in the working of miracles a change is wrought in corporeal matter. Therefore men, by the power of the soul, can change corporeal matter.
reply: The saints are said to work miracles by the power of grace, not of nature. This is clear from what Gregory says in the same place: "Those who are sons of God, in power, as John says--what wonder is there that they should work miracles by that power?"
prompt: It looks like people by the power of their mind can change body stuff. The old teacher says that good people do big change sometimes by talk to the one we ask, sometimes by their power: for example the first friend, by talk to the one we ask, made the dead woman live, and by his words made the person who told a lie die. But in the making of big change a change is made in body stuff. So people by the power of their mind can change body stuff.
response: Good people are said to do big change by the power of help from the one we ask, not by normal ways. This is clear from what the old teacher says in the same talk: Those who are child of the one we ask, with power, as the wise old teacher from a far place says, why would they do big change with that power?

article_title: Whether Sacred Doctrine is a Matter of Argument?
objection: It seems this doctrine is not a matter of argument. For Ambrose says (De Fide 1): "Put arguments aside where faith is sought." But in this doctrine, faith especially is sought: "But these things are written that you may believe" (John 20:31). Therefore sacred doctrine is not a matter of argument.
reply: Although arguments from human reason cannot avail to prove what must be received on faith, nevertheless, this doctrine argues from articles of faith to other truths.
prompt: It seems this teaching to do with God is not a talk about reasons. For the old wise church teacher says (De Fide 1): "Put talk about reasons away where belief is looked for." But in this teaching belief is looked for: "but these things are written so that you may believe". So this teaching to do with God is not a talk about reasons.
response: But talk about reasons from a human head cannot show what you have to take on belief, still this teaching talks from parts of belief to other true things.

article_title: Whether among the demons there is precedence?
objection: Further, there is no precedence where obedience and subjection do not exist. But these cannot be without concord; which is not to be found among the demons, according to the text, "Among the proud there are always contentions" (Prov. 13:10). Therefore there is no precedence among the demons.
reply: The concord of the demons, whereby some obey others, does not arise from mutual friendships, but from their common wickedness whereby they hate men, and fight against God's justice. For it belongs to wicked men to be joined to and subject to those whom they see to be stronger, in order to carry out their own wickedness.
prompt: Also, there is no order where hear and being under are not. But those need get along, and get along is not found with the bad ones, as the old word say "Among the full of self there are always pushes". So there is no order with the bad ones.
response: The getting along of the bad ones where some hear others does not come from being nice to each other but from their same bad that makes them do not like people and hit against what is right. It is usual for bad people to be with and under those they think are big so they can do their own bad things.

article_title: Whether All Things Are Subject to Fate?
objection: Further, Boethius says (De Consol. iv) that fate "is a disposition inherent to changeable things." But all creatures are changeable, and God alone is truly unchangeable, as stated above (Q. 9, A. 2). Therefore fate is in all things.
reply: Although all creatures are in some way changeable, yet some of them do not proceed from changeable created causes. And these, therefore, are not subject to fate, as stated above.
prompt: Also the old teacher says that the thing that makes things happen is a way that is inside things that can change. But all things that live can change, and the big good is the only one that can not change, as said before. So the thing that makes things happen is in all things.
response: Even though all things that live can change in some way, some of them do not come from things that can change that were made. So those are not under the thing that makes things happen, as said before.

article_title: Whether the Will of God Is Always Fulfilled?
objection: Further, since the will of God is the first cause, it does not exclude intermediate causes. But the effect of a first cause may be hindered by a defect of a secondary cause; as the effect of the motive power may be hindered by the weakness of the limb. Therefore the effect of the divine will may be hindered by a defect of the secondary causes. The will of God, therefore, is not always fulfilled.
reply: A first cause can be hindered in its effect by deficiency in the secondary cause, when it is not the universal first cause, including within itself all causes; for then the effect could in no way escape its order. And thus it is with the will of God, as said above.
prompt: Also, because the want of the big person is the first reason, it does not stop other reasons. But the result of a first reason can be stopped by a problem in another reason, like the result of a push can be stopped by the weak arm. So the result of the big person want can be stopped by a problem in other reasons. The big person want is not always done.
response: A first reason can be stopped in its result by a problem in another reason when it is not the first reason that has all reasons inside it; for then the result could not get out of its way. So the same is true for the big person want, as said before.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, some animals at birth have sufficient strength to use their members. But man is nobler than other animals. Therefore much more is it natural to man to have strength to use his members at birth; and thus it appears to be a punishment of sin that he has not that strength.
reply: The fact that some animals have the use of their limbs immediately after birth, is due, not to their superiority, since more perfect animals are not so endowed; but to the dryness of the brain, and to the operations proper to such animals being imperfect, so that a small amount of strength suffices them.
prompt: Also, some animals when they are born have enough strong to use their body parts. But people are more good than other animals. So it is more normal for people to have strong to use their body parts when they are born; and it seems like a bad thing because of a bad act that they do not have that strong.
response: The fact that some animals can use their body parts right after they are born is not because they are better, since better animals do not have that. It is because their brain is dry and the way they work is not perfect, so a little strong is enough for them.

article_title: Whether the Angels Understand by Composing and Dividing?
objection: It would seem that the angels understand by composing and dividing. For, where there is multiplicity of things understood, there is composition of the same, as is said in De Anima iii, text. 21. But there is a multitude of things understood in the angelic mind; because angels apprehend different things by various species, and not all at one time. Therefore there is composition and division in the angel's mind.
reply: Not every multitude of things understood causes composition, but a multitude of such things understood that one of them is attributed to, or denied of, another. When an angel apprehends the nature of anything, he at the same time understands whatever can be either attributed to it, or denied of it. Hence, in apprehending a nature, he by one simple perception grasps all that we can learn by composing and dividing.
prompt: It seems that heaven people understand by putting together and breaking apart. Because where there are many things known, there is putting together of them, as said in the old book about the mind. But there are many things known in a heaven person mind; because heaven people know different things by different kinds, and not all at once. So there is putting together and breaking apart in a heaven person mind.
response: Not every many things known makes putting together, but only when many things known has one that is said to be part of another, or said not to be part of another. When a heaven person knows what something is, they at the same time know all things that can be said to be part of it, or said not to be part of it. So, by one simple look, they get all that we can learn by putting together and breaking apart.

article_title: Whether Creation Is Anything in the Creature?
objection: It would seem that creation is not anything in the creature. For as creation taken in a passive sense is attributed to the creature, so creation taken in an active sense is attributed to the Creator. But creation taken actively is not anything in the Creator, because otherwise it would follow that in God there would be something temporal. Therefore creation taken passively is not anything in the creature.
reply: Creation signified actively means the divine action, which is God's essence, with a relation to the creature. But in God relation to the creature is not a real relation, but only a relation of reason; whereas the relation of the creature to God is a real relation, as was said above (Q. 13, A. 7) in treating of the divine names.
prompt: It would seem that making is not any part of the thing with life. When we talk about making as something that is done to the thing, we say it is for the thing; and when we talk about making as something that is done by the big one who makes, we say it is for the big one who makes. But making that is done by the big one who makes is not any part of the big one who makes, because if it were then there would be some time part in the big one who makes. So the making that is done to the thing is not any part of the thing with life.
response: When making is done it means the big one who makes does an act, which is the big one who makes what a big one really is, with a way to the thing with life. But in the big one who makes the way to the thing with life is not a real way, but only a way in thought; while the way of the thing with life to the big one who makes is a real way, as we said before.

article_title: Whether the Will of God Is the Cause of Things?
objection: It seems that the will of God is not the cause of things. For Dionysius says (Div. Nom. iv, 1): "As our sun, not by reason nor by pre-election, but by its very being, enlightens all things that can participate in its light, so the divine good by its very essence pours the rays of goodness upon everything that exists." But every voluntary agent acts by reason and pre-election. Therefore God does not act by will; and so His will is not the cause of things.
reply: Dionysius in these words does not intend to exclude election from God absolutely; but only in a certain sense, in so far, that is, as He communicates His goodness not merely to certain things, but to all; and as election implies a certain distinction.
prompt: It looks like the want of the great one does not make things. For the old teacher says our sun makes all things that can be in its light not because it thinks or wants but because it is so the good that is from the great one also puts good light on all that is. Any one that can want does by think and want. So the great one does not do by want and so the want of the great one does not make things.
response: the old teacher in these words does not mean to leave out want from the great one at all but only in one way that is as he puts good not just to some things but to all and as want means a different

article_title: Whether There Is Procession in God?
objection: Further, everything which proceeds differs from that whence it proceeds. But in God there is no diversity; but supreme simplicity. Therefore in God there is no procession.
reply: Whatever proceeds by way of outward procession is necessarily distinct from the source whence it proceeds, whereas, whatever proceeds within by an intelligible procession is not necessarily distinct; indeed, the more perfectly it proceeds, the more closely it is one with the source whence it proceeds. For it is clear that the more a thing is understood, the more closely is the intellectual conception joined and united to the intelligent agent; since the intellect by the very act of understanding is made one with the object understood. Thus, as the divine intelligence is the very supreme perfection of God (Q. 14, A. 2), the divine Word is of necessity perfectly one with the source whence He proceeds, without any kind of diversity.
prompt: Also everything that come out is different from the thing it come out from. But in god there is no many part; only one plain thing. So in god there is no come out.
response: Things that come out away are every time different from the thing they come out from. But things that come out in a clear mind way do not have to be different; the most good come out is near to the thing it comes out from. The more we know a thing, the more the knowing and the thing are together. Since the mind that knows is the most good of god, the god talk has to be very one with the start it comes out from, with no different.

article_title: Whether Corporeal Matter Obeys the Mere Will of an Angel?
objection: It would seem that corporeal matter obeys the mere will of an angel. For the power of an angel excels the power of the soul. But corporeal matter obeys a conception of the soul; for the body of man is changed by a conception of the soul as regards heat and cold, and sometimes even as regards health and sickness. Therefore much more is corporeal matter changed by a conception of an angel.
reply: Our soul is united to the body as the form; and so it is not surprising for the body to be formally changed by the soul's concept; especially as the movement of the sensitive appetite, which is accompanied with a certain bodily change, is subject to the command of reason. An angel, however, has not the same connection with natural bodies; and hence the argument does not hold.
prompt: It looks like body stuff does what just want of a being from a far place. Because the power of a being from a far place is more than the power of the inside part. But body stuff does what an idea of the inside part says; because a man body is made different by an idea of the inside part about hot and cold, and now and then about well and ill. So body stuff is made more different by an idea of a being from a far place.
response: Our inside part is together with the body as its shape; so it is not new that the body is made different by the idea of the inside part; for example as the motion of the feeling want, which comes with a certain body change, is under the order of the mind. A being from a far place, however, does not have the same tie with real body stuff; and so the point does not work.

article_title: Whether God Is the Final Cause of All Things?
objection: Further, the final cause is the first of causes. If, therefore, God is the efficient cause and the final cause, it follows that before and after exist in Him; which is impossible.
reply: Since God is the efficient, the exemplar and the final cause of all things, and since primary matter is from Him, it follows that the first principle of all things is one in reality. But this does not prevent us from mentally considering many things in Him, some of which come into our mind before others.
prompt: Also, the last cause is the first cause. So if God is the cause that makes and the last cause, then earlier and later would be inside God; that cannot happen.
response: Because God is the cause that makes, the example, and the last cause of all things, and because the first stuff comes from God, the first idea of all things is one in real. But this does not stop us from thinking about many things in God, some of which we think of first and others later.

article_title: Whether Any Name Can Be Applied to God Substantially?
objection: Further, Dionysius says (Div. Nom. i): "You will find a chorus of holy doctors addressed to the end of distinguishing clearly and praiseworthily the divine processions in the denomination of God." Thus the names applied by the holy doctors in praising God are distinguished according to the divine processions themselves. But what expresses the procession of anything, does not signify its essence. Therefore the names applied to God are not said of Him substantially.
reply: In the significance of names, that from which the name is derived is different sometimes from what it is intended to signify, as for instance, this name "stone" [lapis] is imposed from the fact that it hurts the foot [loedit pedem], but it is not imposed to signify that which hurts the foot, but rather to signify a certain kind of body; otherwise everything that hurts the foot would be a stone [*This refers to the Latin etymology of the word lapis, which has no place in English]. So we must say that these kinds of divine names are imposed from the divine processions; for as according to the diverse processions of their perfections, creatures are the representations of God, although in an imperfect manner; so likewise our intellect knows and names God according to each kind of procession; but nevertheless these names are not imposed to signify the procession themselves, as if when we say "God lives," the sense were, "life proceeds from Him"; but to signify the principle itself of things, in so far as life pre-exists in Him, although it pre-exists in Him in a more eminent way than can be understood or signified.
prompt: the wise old teacher says that you will hear many good teachers talk in order to make clear and good the ways of God in the names we give God. thus the words we use for God when we praise Him are made different as the ways of God themselves. but what shows the way of something does not show what it really is. so the words we use for God are not said about Him in the real way.
response: Sometimes a word comes from one thing but is used to talk about a different thing. For example we call a hard part that can hurt a foot a stone, not because the foot hurt thing is a stone, but because the hard part can hurt a foot. If we called everything that hurts a foot a stone we would be wrong. In the same way the words we use for God come from the ways God moves. The many ways God is good make all things show a picture of God even if they are not good. Our mind use a word for each way we see God but the word is not meant to mean the way it. When we say God lives we do not mean that life moves out of God we mean that the real start of all life is God even if we cannot see it or say it.

article_title: Whether an Angel Is Composed of Matter and Form?
objection: Further, form is act. So what is form only is pure act. But an angel is not pure act, for this belongs to God alone. Therefore an angel is not form only, but has a form in matter.
reply: Although there is no composition of matter and form in an angel, yet there is act and potentiality. And this can be made evident if we consider the nature of material things which contain a twofold composition. The first is that of form and matter, whereby the nature is constituted. Such a composite nature is not its own existence but existence is its act. Hence the nature itself is related to its own existence as potentiality to act. Therefore if there be no matter, and supposing that the form itself subsists without matter, there nevertheless still remains the relation of the form to its very existence, as of potentiality to act. And such a kind of composition is understood to be in the angels; and this is what some say, that an angel is composed of, "whereby he is," and "what is," or "existence," and "what is," as Boethius says. For "what is," is the form itself subsisting; and the existence itself is whereby the substance is; as the running is whereby the runner runs. But in God "existence" and "what is" are not different as was explained above (Q. 3, A. 4). Hence God alone is pure act.
prompt: Also, shape is doing. So if something is only shape, it is just doing. But a spirit being is not just doing, because only god is just doing. So a spirit being is not only shape, but has a shape in stuff.
response: Even if a spirit being does not have a mix of stuff and shape, it still has doing and can become. We can see this by looking at stuff things, which have two parts. The first part is shape and stuff, which put the thing together. That mix is not the thing's own being; the thing's being is its doing. So the thing is connected to its own being like can become is connected to doing. So if there is no stuff and the shape lives by itself, there is still a link of the shape to its own being, like can become to doing. This type of link is said to be in spirit beings; some say a spirit being is made of "what it is" and "what it does", as the old wise man from a far place says. "What it is" is the shape itself; "what it does" is the being, like running is to a runner. But in god "what it does" and "what it is" are not different. So only god is just doing.

article_title: (no title)
objection: Further, measure is homogeneous with the thing measured. But God is the first measure of all beings. Therefore God is homogeneous with creatures; and thus a word may be applied univocally to God and to creatures.
reply: God is not the measure proportioned to things measured; hence it is not necessary that God and creatures should be in the same genus. The arguments adduced in the contrary sense prove indeed that these names are not predicated univocally of God and creatures; yet they do not prove that they are predicated equivocally.
prompt: Also, a size is the same kind as the thing it looks at. But the big one is the first size for all things. So the big one is the same kind as living things; and a word can be used in the same way for the big one and for living things.
response: The big one is not the size that fits the things it looks at, so we do not have to put the big one and living things in the same group. The reasons that people give for the other side show that the same word is not used in just the same way for the big one and for living things, but they do not show that the word is used in a very different way.

article_title: Whether the Inequality of Things Is from God?
objection: It would seem that the inequality of things is not from God. For it belongs to the best to produce the best. But among things that are best, one is not greater than another. Therefore, it belongs to God, Who is the Best, to make all things equal.
reply: It is part of the best agent to produce an effect which is best in its entirety; but this does not mean that He makes every part of the whole the best absolutely, but in proportion to the whole; in the case of an animal, for instance, its goodness would be taken away if every part of it had the dignity of an eye. Thus, therefore, God also made the universe to be best as a whole, according to the mode of a creature; whereas He did not make each single creature best, but one better than another. And therefore we find it said of each creature, "God saw the light that it was good" (Gen. 1:4); and in like manner of each one of the rest. But of all together it is said, "God saw all the things that He had made, and they were very good" (Gen. 1:31).
prompt: It looks like the not same of thing is not from the great good one. For it is for the most good to make the most good. But in the most good thing one is not more than else. So it is for the great good one who is the most good to make all thing the same.
response: So the great good one also made the world to be most good as all in the way of an animal, but He did not make each one animal the most good, but one more good than else. And we see it said of each animal, the great good one saw the light and said it was good. And also together it is said, the great good one saw all the thing He made and they were very good.
